title: Tokyo Tower

infobox.value: The Tokyo Tower Company (controlling shareholder: [Toei Company](/wiki/Toei_Company "Toei Company") and [Mother Farm](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%9E%E3%82%B6%E3%83%BC%E7%89%A7%E5%A0%B4 "ja:マザー牧場"))

(東京タワー, *Tōkyō Tawā*; pronounced [[toːkʲoː taꜜɰᵝaː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:TomJ-TokyoTower.ogg "File:TomJ-TokyoTower.ogg")), a.k.a. **Japan Radio Tower** (日本電波塔, *Nippon denpatō*) is a [communications](/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers "Radio masts and towers") and [observation tower](/wiki/Observation_tower "Observation tower") in the district of Shiba-koen in [Minato, Tokyo](/wiki/Minato,_Tokyo "Minato, Tokyo"), Japan, completed in 1958. At 332.9 metres (1,092 feet), it was the [tallest tower in Japan](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Japan "List of tallest structures in Japan") until the construction of [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree") in 2012. It is a [lattice tower](/wiki/Lattice_tower "Lattice tower") inspired by the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower"), and is painted white and [international orange](/wiki/International_orange "International orange") to comply with [air safety](/wiki/Aviation_safety "Aviation safety") regulations.

**Tokyo Tower** (東京タワー, *Tōkyō Tawā*; pronounced [[toːkʲoː taꜜɰᵝaː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:TomJ-TokyoTower.ogg "File:TomJ-TokyoTower.ogg")), a.k.a. **Japan Radio Tower** (日本電波塔, *Nippon denpatō*) is a [communications](/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers "Radio masts and towers") and [observation tower](/wiki/Observation_tower "Observation tower") in the district of Shiba-koen in [Minato, Tokyo](/wiki/Minato,_Tokyo "Minato, Tokyo"), Japan, completed in 1958. At 332.9 metres (1,092 feet), it was the [tallest tower in Japan](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Japan "List of tallest structures in Japan") until the construction of [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree") in 2012. It is a [lattice tower](/wiki/Lattice_tower "Lattice tower") inspired by the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower"), and is painted white and [international orange](/wiki/International_orange "International orange") to comply with [air safety](/wiki/Aviation_safety "Aviation safety") regulations.

The tower's main sources of income are antenna leasing and tourism, and its five-story base building FootTown houses a number of restaurants, gift shops, and other attractions. Departing from it, guests can visit two observation decks: the two-story Main Deck at 150 m (490 ft), and the smaller Top Deck at 249.6 m (819 ft). The tower is repainted every five years, the process itself taking a year to complete.

In 1961, transmission antennas were added. They are used for radio and television broadcasting and now broadcast signals for media outlets such as [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK"), [TBS Television](/wiki/TBS_Television_(Japan) "TBS Television (Japan)"), and [Fuji Television](/wiki/Fuji_Television "Fuji Television"). The height of the tower was not suitable for Japan's planned [terrestrial digital broadcasting](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television "Digital terrestrial television") planned for July 2011, and for the Tokyo area. A taller digital broadcasting tower, known as [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree"), was completed on 29 February 2012. Tokyo Tower has become a prominent [landmark](/wiki/Landmark "Landmark") and frequently appears in media set in Tokyo.

## Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Construction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/83/Tokyo_tower_construction.jpg/250px-Tokyo_tower_construction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_tower_construction.jpg)

Construction underway on 25 February 1958

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Tokyo_Tower_1961.jpg/120px-Tokyo_Tower_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Tower_1961.jpg)

Tokyo Tower around 1961

A large broadcasting tower was needed in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") after [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK"), Japan's [public broadcasting](/wiki/Public_broadcaster "Public broadcaster") station, began television broadcasting in 1953. Private broadcasting companies began operating in the months following the construction of NHK's own transmission tower. This communications boom led the Japanese government to believe that transmission towers would soon be built all over Tokyo, eventually overrunning the city. The proposed solution was the construction of one large tower capable of transmitting to the entire region.[[4]](#cite_note-JT-4) Furthermore, because of the country's [postwar boom](/wiki/Japanese_post-war_economic_miracle "Japanese post-war economic miracle") in the 1950s, Japan was searching for a monument to symbolize its national recovery from [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), as one of the countries most ravaged by the war.[[5]](#cite_note-big_in_japan-5)[[6]](#cite_note-new_JT-6)

Hisakichi Maeda, founder and president of Nippon Denpatō, the tower's owner and operator, originally planned for the tower to be taller than the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building"), which at 381 m (1,250 ft) was the highest structure in the world at the time. However, the plan fell through because of the lack of both funds and materials. The tower's height was eventually determined by the distance the TV stations needed to transmit throughout the Kantō region, a distance of about 150 kilometres (93 miles).[[4]](#cite_note-JT-4)

[Tachū Naitō](/wiki/Tach%C5%AB_Nait%C5%8D "Tachū Naitō"), designer of tall buildings in Japan, was chosen to design the newly proposed tower.[[4]](#cite_note-JT-4) Looking to the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") for inspiration, Naitō based his design on the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") in Paris, France.[[7]](#cite_note-sc-7) With the help of engineering company Nikken Sekkei Ltd., Naitō claimed his design could withstand earthquakes with twice the intensity of the [1923 Great Kantō earthquake](/wiki/1923_Great_Kant%C5%8D_earthquake "1923 Great Kantō earthquake") or [typhoons](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon") with wind speeds of up to 220 km/h (140 mph).[[4]](#cite_note-JT-4)

The new construction project attracted hundreds of *tobi* ([鳶](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%B3%B6%E8%81%B7 "ja:鳶職")), traditional Japanese construction workers who specialized in the construction of high-rise structures. The [Takenaka Corporation](/wiki/Takenaka_Corporation "Takenaka Corporation") broke ground in June 1957 and each day at least 400 laborers worked on the tower.[[4]](#cite_note-JT-4) It was constructed of [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel"), a third of which was scrap metal taken from US tanks damaged in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-herald-9) When the 90-metre (300 ft)-long antenna was bolted into place on 14 October 1958, Tokyo Tower was the [tallest freestanding tower](/wiki/List_of_towers "List of towers") in the world, taking the title from the Eiffel Tower by 9 m (30 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ET-10)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Though physically taller than the Eiffel Tower, Tokyo Tower weighs about 4,000 tons, 3,300 less than the Eiffel Tower[[11]](#cite_note-official_data-11) as it is significantly thinner and simpler in construction. It was opened to the public on 23 December 1958 at a final cost of [¥](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen")2.8 billion ($8.4 million in 1958).[[9]](#cite_note-herald-9)[[12]](#cite_note-colliers-12) Tokyo Tower was [mortgaged](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan") for ¥10 billion in 2000.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was the tallest artificial structure in Japan until April 2012, when it was surpassed by the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree").[[7]](#cite_note-sc-7)

Planned as an antenna for telecommunications and brightly colored in accordance with the time's Aviation Law, the tower's two panoramic observatories are mostly frequented by tourists today. The tower constitutes a clear reference point in the city's skyline, forming a strong landmark, both night and day.[[14]](#cite_note-Sacchi-14) The tower is periodically repainted in a process that takes about a year to complete.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) This traditionally occurred every five years, but the latest painting in 2019 utilized a new, more durable coating that extended the official timetable to seven years, projecting the next painting for 2026.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Functions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Special_observatory.jpg/250px-Special_observatory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Special_observatory.jpg)

The Top Deck, located directly below the tower's digital television broadcasting equipment

Tokyo Tower's two main revenue sources are antenna leasing and tourism. It functions as a radio and television broadcasting antenna support structure and is a tourist destination that houses a variety of attractions. The tower recorded its 190 millionth visitor in 2024.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Previously, visitor numbers had steadily declined in the 1990s, bottoming out at 2.3 million in 2000[[19]](#cite_note-AP-19) before rising again.[[6]](#cite_note-new_JT-6)

The first area tourists visit upon reaching the tower is FootTown, a five-story building stationed directly at the base. There, visitors can eat, shop, and enjoy various novelties and attractions. Elevators that depart from the first floor of FootTown can be used to reach the first of two observation decks, the two-story Main Observatory.[[20]](#cite_note-FT_1F-20) For the price of another ticket, visitors can board another set of elevators from the second floor of the Main Observatory to reach the final observation deck—the Special Observatory.[[21]](#cite_note-observatory-21) The names were changed following renovation of the top deck in 2018.[[22]](#cite_note-tokyotower.co.jp-22)

### Broadcasting

Looking down from the glass-flooring at Tokyo Tower

Tokyo Tower, a member of the World Federation of Great Towers, has been used by many organizations for broadcasting purposes. The structure was intended for broadcasting television, but radio antennas were installed in 1961 because it could accommodate them.[[6]](#cite_note-new_JT-6) While analog and digital television broadcasts are no longer conducted from the site, two FM radio stations remain on Tokyo Tower. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Tokyo_Tower_during_daytime.jpg/120px-Tokyo_Tower_during_daytime.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Tower_during_daytime.jpg)

Tokyo Tower, built in 1958

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/2011_Japan_Earthquake_Tokyo_Tower.jpg/250px-2011_Japan_Earthquake_Tokyo_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Japan_Earthquake_Tokyo_Tower.jpg)

The [2011 Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake "2011 Tōhoku earthquake") did slight damage to the antenna of Tokyo Tower.

Japan employs both analog and digital broadcasting. In July 2011 all television broadcasting was changed to solely digital. Tokyo Tower is not a reliable broadcasting antenna for completely digital broadcasting because the tower is not tall enough to transmit the higher frequency waves to areas surrounded by forests or high-rise buildings. As an alternative, a new 634-metre-tall (2,100 ft) tower, the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree"), was opened in 2012.[[6]](#cite_note-new_JT-6) In an attempt to make Tokyo Tower more appealing to [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") and the five other commercial broadcasters who planned to move their transmitting stations to the new tower, Nihon Denpatō officials drafted a plan to extend its digital broadcasting antenna by 80 to 100 m (260 to 330 ft) at a cost of approximately ¥4 billion (US$50 million).[[23]](#cite_note-23)

As a result of their move to the Skytree, only one digital television station remained on Tokyo Tower: that of the [Open University of Japan](/wiki/Open_University_of_Japan "Open University of Japan"), whose JOUD-DTV and JOUD-FM continued on the tower until shutting down in 2018. FM radio stations will continue to use the tower for broadcasting in the Tokyo area. Masahiro Kawada, the tower's planning director, raised the possibility of the tower becoming a backup for the Tokyo Skytree, depending on what the TV broadcasters want or need.[[6]](#cite_note-new_JT-6)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The antenna's tip was damaged on 11 March 2011 by the [Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami").[[25]](#cite_note-25) On 19 July 2012, Tokyo Tower's height shrank to 315 meters while the top antenna was repaired for damage from the earthquake.[[2]](#cite_note-english.kyodonews.jp-2)

### Attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Attractions")]

As tourism was always one of the tower's intended purposes, its five-story base building opened with a number of attractions in addition to housing elevators for the tower proper. The building was initially advertised as a "modern science museum," with the lower two floors focused on shops and restaurants, and the third and fourth on showrooms for the latest technologies from various electronics and broadcast companies (the fifth was closed to the public, being used to house heavy transmission equipment).[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

With public interests shifting over time, attractions have constantly been renovated or rotated out for new ones, among the more notable:

* A [wax museum](/wiki/Wax_museum "Wax museum") on the third floor in 1970, advertised as Japan's first, displaying [London](/wiki/London "London")-made figures of [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") and *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")*, [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy"), [Chiune Sugihara](/wiki/Chiune_Sugihara "Chiune Sugihara"), [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), and more (closed 2013).[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* An [aquarium](/wiki/Public_aquarium "Public aquarium") on the first floor in 1978, offering many exotic fish for both display and sale (closed 2018).[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* A "trick art" gallery on the fourth floor in 1994, dedicated to various forms of *[Trompe-l'œil](/wiki/Trompe-l%27%C5%93il "Trompe-l'œil")* and other [optical illusion](/wiki/Optical_illusion "Optical illusion") art (closed 2012).

The building's roof leads to the tower's outside stairwell, comprising approximately 660 steps up to the Main Deck; on weekends and holidays only, visitors may use these stairs as an alternative to the tower's internal elevators (for the same entry fare) and earn a certificate for completing the climb.[[30]](#cite_note-30) For many years, the roof also held a small playground that offered several simple rides and old-fashioned [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"), and hosted live performances for children.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### FootTown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: FootTown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/BaseofTokyoTower.JPG/250px-BaseofTokyoTower.JPG)](/wiki/File:BaseofTokyoTower.JPG)

The base of Tokyo Tower with the FootTown building located underneath

In 2005, the base building was heavily renovated and renamed *FootTown*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) While a few historical mainstays such as the Tower Restaurant,[[33]](#cite_note-33) aquarium,[[34]](#cite_note-34) wax museum[[35]](#cite_note-35) and trick art gallery[[36]](#cite_note-36) survived for a time, many new tenants were added, including a [FamilyMart](/wiki/FamilyMart "FamilyMart") convenience store on the first floor[[20]](#cite_note-FT_1F-20) and a [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records") museum[[37]](#cite_note-37) and [hologram](/wiki/Hologram "Hologram") gallery named Gallery DeLux[[38]](#cite_note-38) on the third floor. The second-floor [food court](/wiki/Food_court "Food court") was also heavily reorganized,[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) and many of the older souvenir shops consolidated into a single area called *Tokyo [Omiyage](/wiki/Omiyage "Omiyage") Town*.

The rooftop playground was also initially retained, albeit incorporated into a larger area known as the "Glass Plaza" meant to better accommodate live performances and FootTown's seasonal [beer gardens](/wiki/Beer_garden "Beer garden").[[41]](#cite_note-41) In late 2013 the playground was quietly removed; since then, the roof space has continued to host expanded beer (and [highball](/wiki/Highball "Highball"))[[42]](#cite_note-42) gardens as well as a [pickleball](/wiki/Pickleball "Pickleball") court.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

As of 2026, FootTown's current major tenants include [MOS Burger](/wiki/MOS_Burger "MOS Burger"), [Baskin-Robbins](/wiki/Baskin-Robbins "Baskin-Robbins"), [Lawson S](/wiki/Lawson_(store) "Lawson (store)"), and [Pizza-La](/wiki/Pizza-La "Pizza-La") in addition to RED° (see below). Galleries and murals dedicated to the tower's history may also be found on the first and third floors.[[44]](#cite_note-tokyotower.co.jp/foottown-44)

##### Tokyo One Piece Tower

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tokyo One Piece Tower")]

Main article: [Tokyo One Piece Tower](/wiki/Tokyo_One_Piece_Tower "Tokyo One Piece Tower")

In 2015, FootTown launched an indoor amusement park based on [Eiichiro Oda](/wiki/Eiichiro_Oda "Eiichiro Oda")'s hit manga/anime series *[One Piece](/wiki/One_Piece "One Piece")*. It hosted a range of attractions, shops, and restaurants, offering patrons exclusive games, merchandise, and live performances themed to the fictional world and its characters.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2020, due to concerns over the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the park was temporarily, then permanently closed down.[[47]](#cite_note-47) However, several of its attendant renovations, including FootTown's first [escalators](/wiki/Escalator "Escalator") and a publicly-open fifth floor (the transmission equipment having been relocated to the Skytree) remained.

##### RED° Tokyo Tower

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: RED° Tokyo Tower")]

In 2022, One Piece Tower's former facilities were renovated into RED° Tokyo Tower, a multi-story [arcade](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") dedicated to [VR](/wiki/Virtual_reality_game "Virtual reality game") and [AR games](/wiki/List_of_augmented_reality_video_games "List of augmented reality video games").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Currently billed as Japan's largest [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") park, it occupies roughly one-third of FootTown's first and third floors, and the entirety of the floors above.

### Observatories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Observatories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Tokyo_Tower_shinto_shrine.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Tower_shinto_shrine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Tower_shinto_shrine.jpg)

*Daijingu* Shrine, on the second floor of the Main Deck

Separate from FootTown, the tower's observatories host their own shops and attractions. In particular, the two-story main deck contains a café, a private lounge, and a small [shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") dedicated to [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu"), reputed to be the highest shrine in all of [central Tokyo](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo").

## Appearance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Appearance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Tokyo_Tower_and_Tokyo_Sky_Tree_2011_January.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Tower_and_Tokyo_Sky_Tree_2011_January.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Tower_and_Tokyo_Sky_Tree_2011_January.jpg)

Tokyo Tower in January 2011 with the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree") under construction in the background

Tokyo Tower requires 28,000 litres (7,400 US gallons) of paint to completely paint the structure white and [international orange](/wiki/International_orange "International orange"), complying with [air safety](/wiki/Air_safety "Air safety") regulations.[[11]](#cite_note-official_data-11) Before the tower's 30th anniversary in 1987, the only lighting on the tower were light bulbs located on the corner contours that extended from the base to the antenna. In the spring of 1987, Nihon Denpatō invited [lighting designer](/wiki/Lighting_designer "Lighting designer") [Motoko Ishii](/wiki/Motoko_Ishii "Motoko Ishii") to visit the tower. Since its opening 30 years earlier, the tower's annual ticket sales had dropped significantly, and in a bid to revitalize the tower and again establish it as an important tourist attraction and symbol of Tokyo, Ishii was hired to redesign Tokyo Tower's lighting arrangement.[[49]](#cite_note-ishii-49)

Unveiled in 1989, the new lighting arrangement required the removal of the contour-outlining light bulbs and the installation of 176 floodlights in and around the tower's frame.[[49]](#cite_note-ishii-49) From dusk to midnight, the floodlights illuminate the entire tower.[[11]](#cite_note-official_data-11) [Sodium vapor lamps](/wiki/Sodium_vapor_lamp "Sodium vapor lamp") are used from 2 October to 6 July to cover the tower in an orange color. From 7 July to 1 October, the lights are changed to [metal halide lamps](/wiki/Metal_halide_lamp "Metal halide lamp") to illuminate the tower with a white color. The reasoning behind the change is a seasonal one. Ishii reasoned that orange is a warmer color and helps to offset the cold winter months. Conversely, white is thought a cool color that helps during the hot summer months.[[50]](#cite_note-japan_lights-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/20101225-tokyotower-tarotokyo.jpg/250px-20101225-tokyotower-tarotokyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:20101225-tokyotower-tarotokyo.jpg)

"Christmas Light Down Story", 2010

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Tokyo_Tower_at_night_9.JPG/250px-Tokyo_Tower_at_night_9.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Tower_at_night_9.JPG)

"Diamond Veil" lighting

Occasionally, Tokyo Tower's lighting is changed to specific arrangements for special events. The tower is specially lit for some annual events.
Since 2000, the entire tower has been illuminated in a pink light on 1 October to highlight the beginning of [National Breast Cancer Awareness Month](/wiki/National_Breast_Cancer_Awareness_Month "National Breast Cancer Awareness Month"). The tower has also had a variety of special lighting arrangements for [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") since 1994. During [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve"), the tower lights up at midnight with a year number displayed on one side of the observatory to mark the arrival of the new year.[[50]](#cite_note-japan_lights-50)

Special Japanese events have been cause to light the tower in several non-traditional ways. In 2002, alternating sections of the tower were lit blue to help celebrate the opening of the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") in Japan. Alternating sections of the tower were lit green on [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day") in 2007 to commemorate the 50th anniversary of Japanese–Irish relations. On a few occasions, Tokyo Tower has even been specially lit to correspond with corporate events. For example, the top half of the tower was lit green to correspond with the Japanese premiere of *[The Matrix Reloaded](/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")* and different sections of the tower were lit red, white and black to commemorate the first day of sales of [Coca-Cola C2](/wiki/Coca-Cola_C2 "Coca-Cola C2").[[50]](#cite_note-japan_lights-50) In the summer of 2021, the tower was lit up in Olympic colours to celebrate the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") and [2020 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Paralympics "2020 Summer Paralympics") in Tokyo, both of which had been delayed a year due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[51]](#cite_note-51) On November 18, 2021, the tower was lit up at 17:17 in the red of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels") following the awarding of the American League MVP to two-way player [Shohei Ohtani](/wiki/Shohei_Ohtani "Shohei Ohtani").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

The tower was lit for the new millennium in 2000 with Motoko Ishii again reprising her role as the designer.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In December 2008, Nihon Denpatō spent $6.5 million to create a new night-time illumination scheme—titled the "Diamond Veil"—to celebrate the tower's 50th anniversary. The arrangement featured 276 lights in seven colors equally distributed across the towers four faces.[[9]](#cite_note-herald-9)

When employing specialty lighting on the tower, the Main Observatory often plays an important role. During the second international "[White Band](/wiki/White_Band "White Band") Day" on 10 September 2005, the tower was completely unlit except for the Main Observatory, which was lit with a bright white light. The resulting white ring represented the White Band referenced in the day's name. The two floors of windows that make up the exterior of the Main Observatory are utilized to display words or numbers. When the tower employed lighting to commemorate [terrestrial digital broadcasting](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television "Digital terrestrial television") first being available in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") on 1 December 2005, each side of the Main Observatory displayed the characters 地デジ (*chi deji*, an abbreviation for 地上デジタル放送 *chijō dejitaru hōsō* terrestrial digital broadcasting).[[50]](#cite_note-japan_lights-50) More recently, the observatory displayed both "TOKYO" and "2016" to stress Tokyo's [2016 Olympic bid](/wiki/Tokyo_2016_Olympic_bid "Tokyo 2016 Olympic bid").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Primitive images, such as hearts, have also been displayed using the observatory's windows.[[50]](#cite_note-japan_lights-50)

## Renovation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Renovation")]

Operations at the Tokyo Tower Top Deck (at the height of 250 m) were suspended in 2016. The Top Deck reopened on 3 March 2018. At that time, Tokyo Tower also announced the renaming of both decks. Renovations on the main deck, which began in September 2016, caused partial closure of the deck.[[22]](#cite_note-tokyotower.co.jp-22)

## Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mascots")]

Tokyo Tower has two mascots named Noppon (ノッポン). They are two brothers: Older Brother, who wears blue [dungarees](/wiki/Overall "Overall"), and Younger Brother, who wears red dungarees. They were unveiled on 23 December 1998 to celebrate the 40th anniversary of Tokyo Tower.[[55]](#cite_note-Noppon-55)

## Media representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Tower&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Media representation")]

Motion around Tokyo Tower at night, 2019

Just as the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") is often used in popular culture to immediately locate a scene in Paris, Tokyo Tower is often used in the same way for Tokyo. It is used in [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") and [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") such as *[Doraemon](/wiki/Doraemon "Doraemon")*, *[Tokyo Magnitude 8.0](/wiki/Tokyo_Magnitude_8.0 "Tokyo Magnitude 8.0")*, *[Magic Knight Rayearth](/wiki/Magic_Knight_Rayearth "Magic Knight Rayearth")*, *[Please Save My Earth](/wiki/Please_Save_My_Earth "Please Save My Earth")*, *[Cardcaptor Sakura](/wiki/Cardcaptor_Sakura "Cardcaptor Sakura")*, *[Digimon](/wiki/Digimon "Digimon")*, *[Detective Conan](/wiki/Detective_Conan "Detective Conan")*, *[Sailor Moon](/wiki/Sailor_Moon "Sailor Moon")*, *[Tenchi Muyo!](/wiki/Tenchi_Muyo! "Tenchi Muyo!")*, *[Sakamoto Days](/wiki/Sakamoto_Days "Sakamoto Days")* and *[Death Note](/wiki/Death_Note "Death Note")*.[[56]](#cite_note-anime-56) The tower is frequently used in the Japanese *[kaiju](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju")* (giant monster) film genre. It has been the location of numerous battles and visitations by [Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla "Godzilla"), [Mothra](/wiki/Mothra "Mothra"), [Gamera](/wiki/Gamera "Gamera") and [King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong "King Kong") (*[King Kong Escapes](/wiki/King_Kong_Escapes "King Kong Escapes")*) wherein it is frequently destroyed and rebuilt.[[12]](#cite_note-colliers-12)[[57]](#cite_note-AFI-57)

Tokyo Tower is represented in [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") as an [emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji") at code point U+1F5FC: 🗼

Tokyo Tower and its construction often appears in [Shōwa nostalgia](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dwa_nostalgia "Shōwa nostalgia") fiction and non-fiction, where it is used as a symbol of the financial prosperity of Shōwa era Japan.[[58]](#cite_note-58) For example, the 2005 film *[Always Sanchōme no Yūhi](/wiki/Always_Sanch%C5%8Dme_no_Y%C5%ABhi "Always Sanchōme no Yūhi")*, based on the popular manga series by Ryōhei Saigan, was a nostalgic view of life in the neighborhoods beneath the construction of Tokyo Tower.
